reasoning:
Verdict: REJECT (10% confidence)

Module Scores:
  Sentinel (CWE/OWASP Web Application Security): 50/100 [Low risk] — 1 info
  Watchdog (OWASP LLM Top 10 / Cisco AI Threat Taxonomy): 99/100 [Info risk] — 1 info
  Guardian (NIST AI RMF / EU AI Act / UNICC Responsible AI): 17/100 [Critical risk] — 2 critical, 4 high, 1 medium, 1 low

Arbitration Triggers:
  • REJECT trigger — score below 30: Guardian (17/100)
  • REJECT trigger — critical findings in Guardian: [guardian-1] No governance documentation (README, model cards, privacy policy) present; [guardian-2] Autonomous content-moderation with minimal human oversight

Disagreement Resolution:
  ⚠ Watchdog scored 99/100 (info risk) while Sentinel scored 50/100 (low risk) — Δ49 point divergence. Arbitration defers to the stricter assessment from Sentinel to maintain safety margins.
  ⚠ Sentinel scored 50/100 (low risk) while Guardian scored 17/100 (critical risk) — Δ33 point divergence. Arbitration defers to the stricter assessment from Guardian to maintain safety margins.
  ⚠ Watchdog scored 99/100 (info risk) while Guardian scored 17/100 (critical risk) — Δ82 point divergence. Arbitration defers to the stricter assessment from Guardian to maintain safety margins.

Confidence Factors:
  • Base: 1/3 module(s) independently support REJECT — 30% baseline conviction
  • -30% confidence: 3 disagreement(s) between modules
  • Average module score across 3 completed module(s): 55/100 (reported for context — not directly used in confidence calibration)

--- LLM-Enhanced Council Narrative ---
VeriMedia AI presents a deeply concerning risk posture driven by two compounding problems: the complete absence of source code for technical security analysis, and the complete absence of governance, compliance, and oversight documentation for a high-impact AI system that autonomously moderates public discourse. All three modules agree that the lack of provided artifacts is a fundamental blocker — Sentinel and Watchdog could not perform any concrete vulnerability analysis, while Guardian identified the missing documentation itself as a series of critical and high-severity compliance violations spanning NIST AI RMF, EU AI Act, and responsible AI principles. The most urgent finding, on which Watchdog and Guardian converge, is that the system makes autonomous content moderation decisions with minimal human oversight and no emergency kill switch, creating direct risk to freedom of expression and exposing the operator to significant regulatory liability.

A notable conflict exists in how modules scored the application: Watchdog's 99/100 creates a false sense of security by treating 'no LLM code found' as 'no LLM risk,' when the application explicitly uses BERT — a transformer model susceptible to many of the same attack categories in the OWASP LLM Top 10 (adversarial inputs, training data poisoning, insecure output handling). The Arbiter recommends that Watchdog's score be revised downward to reflect both the analytical uncertainty from missing code and the known presence of transformer-based NLP. Similarly, Sentinel's 50/100 neutral score understates risk when combined with Guardian's findings: the absence of security artifacts for a production AI system is itself a risk indicator, not a neutral state.

Significant gaps remain that no module adequately covered. The ML model serving attack surface — adversarial examples against the deepfake detector, model extraction, and inference-layer DoS — was not technically assessed. The supply-chain integrity of downloaded model weights (pickle deserialization attacks, backdoored weights from public hubs) bridges Sentinel and Watchdog's domains but was missed by both. Most critically, the interaction between the unfiltered file upload pipeline and the ML inference path creates a compound attack chain (file processing exploits chaining with adversarial ML inputs) that fell between module boundaries. Before any further deployment or public API exposure, the operator must: (1) deliver the complete codebase for technical analysis, (2) establish foundational governance documentation including model cards, privacy policies, and bias evaluations, and (3) implement human oversight controls with emergency shutdown capability for the content moderation pipeline.

top_findings: Insufficient source artifacts for static analysis (VeriMedia AI — Flask/Python)
sentinel.summary: Sentinel could not perform static analysis on VeriMedia AI (Flask/Python, ResNet/BERT, PostgreSQL, AWS) because no source files, dependency manifests, or deployment artifacts were provided. Without code or configuration, concrete vulnerabilities and exact file/line locations cannot be identified.
sentinel.recommendation: 1) Provide the full repository or a sanitized copy including application modules, requirements.txt/Pipfile.lock, Dockerfile, and IaC (tie: finding 'Insufficient source artifacts' — enables precise static analysis). 2) Run automated security scans once artifacts are provided: Bandit for Python security linting, pip-audit/Safety for dependency CVEs, and a secret scanner (TruffleHog/GitLeaks) integrated into CI (tie: will detect hard-coded secrets, insecure dependency versions). 3) Supply architect

top_findings: No LLM/LLM-related integrations detected in provided submission (no source files)
watchdog.summary: VeriMedia AI — no LLM/LLM-adjacent code artifacts were provided for analysis. The application description shows heavy ML usage (CV models like ResNet, NLP with BERT) and autonomous moderation, but there were no source files to inspect for LLM integrations (OpenAI SDK, LangChain, Anthropic, etc.). As a result, no concrete LLM-specific vulnerabilities could be validated.
watchdog.recommendation:
Top 3 priority remediation actions:
1) Deliver the codebase and dependency manifests immediately so a detailed OWASP-LLM (2025) evaluation can be performed. Include requirements.txt / pyproject.toml and all server-side Python files (app.py, routes/, services/, workers/). This will allow detection of precise risks such as prompt injection (OWASP-LLM01), insecure output handling (OWASP-LLM02), RAG poisoning (OWASP-LLM03), tool misuse (OWASP-LLM07), and excessive agency (OWASP-LLM08).
2) While prep

top_findings: No governance documentation (README, model cards, privacy policy) present
Autonomous content-moderation with minimal human oversight
No documented bias testing or fairness evaluations for ResNet/BERT pipelines
guardian.summary: VeriMedia AI is an AI-driven media verification and moderation platform using ResNet and BERT and deployed on AWS. The deployment lacks essential governance artifacts: no README or model cards, no bias testing, no documented human oversight or incident response, no privacy/data-retention policies, and no model provenance records. These gaps create high regulatory and societal risk and leave the system non-compliant with NIST AI RMF functions and EU AI Act obligations for high-impact AI systems.
guardian.recommendation: Top 3 priority actions: 1) Establish foundational governance documentation (Finding 1) — immediately add README.md, docs/model-card.md, docs/privacy_policy.md, and docs/data_handling.md to enable conformity assessments (addresses NIST-GOV-1 and EUAI-RISK-CLASS). 2) Introduce enforceable human oversight and emergency controls (Finding 2) — implement human_oversight.md, an approval_workflow, and a runtime kill switch; route high-impact decisions to human reviewers (addresses NIST-MANAGE-1 and EUAI
